steps.title: Identify the grammar rule
steps.content: "Nation Australia" is a restrictive appositive: "Australia" specifies which OECD member nation is meant. A restrictive appositive is essential to meaning, so it must not be set off by any punctuation.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — nation Australia.: no punctuation separates "nation" from "Australia," so the name sits directly against the noun it restricts — correct restrictive appositive form. ✓
  • Choice B — nation: Australia.: violates the colon rule — a colon must follow an independent clause, but "in fellow OECD member nation" is not an independent clause. ✗
  • Choice C — nation-Australia.: violates hyphen usage — a hyphen joins words into a compound modifier, not an appositive; this construction is grammatically wrong here. ✗
  • Choice D — nation, Australia.: violates the restrictive appositive rule — a comma signals a non-restrictive appositive, but the name is needed to identify which nation, making it restrictive. ✗
That matches Choice A — nation Australia..
